To bridge the gap between the reference points of salient queries and Transformer detectors, we propose SAlient Point-based DETR (SAP-DETR) by treating object detection as a transformation from salient points to instance objects.

To bridge the gap between the reference points of salient queries and Transformer detectors, we propose SAlient Point-based DETR (SAP-DETR) by treating object detection as a transformation from salient points to instance objects.

By rapidly attending to query-specific reference regions and the conditional box edges, SAP-DETR can effectively bridge the gap between the salient point and the query-based Transformer detector with a significant convergency speed.

Salient points are utilized to assign query-specific reference points to object queries, enhancing the accuracy of the detection process.
Unlike center-based methods, salient points restrict the reference location and define the point of the positive query as the salient one, reducing redundant predictions.

The target of keypoint-based approaches is to localize the specific object locations and assign them to the predefined keypoints of the object for box localized training.

SAP-DETR is proposed for promoting model convergency by treating object detection as a transformation from the salient points to the instance objects
The concept of model convergency is achieved through treating object detection as a transformation from salient points to instance objects.

The SAP-DETR explicitly initializes a query-specific reference point for each object query, gradually aggregates them into an instance object, and predicts the distance from each side of the bounding box, contributing to the model convergency.
The SAP-DETR model plays a significant role in the process by treating object detection and initializing query-specific reference points for each object query.
SAP-DETR speedily attends to the query-specific region and other extreme regions from contextual image features, bridging the gap between salient points and the query-based Transformer detector.
Extensive experiments have shown that SAP-DETR achieves superior model convergency speed compared to other approaches, indicating its effectiveness.
Future work for SAP-DETR involves addressing issues related to training with deformable attention, multi-scale features, and negative query design to enhance its performance further.

The point-based design of SAP-DETR brings up remaining issues that need to be resolved, especially in training with deformable attention and multi-scale features, suggesting the need for improvements in this area.
